title: Blindsight and Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)
focus: Neuralink's Blindsight technology, its mechanism of restoring vision, and the anatomy involved.
rationale: Highly specific medical technology with clear anatomical and technological facts suitable for science and tech MCQs.
sectionExcerpt: Neuralink aims to implant the 'Blindsight' chip by 2025. It is a BCI implant that aims to restore vision by bypassing the retina and directly stimulating the visual cortex using a microelectrode array.
evidenceBullets: Blindsight is a BCI implant aiming to restore vision by bypassing the retina.
It directly stimulates the visual cortex, the outermost layer of nerve cell tissues responsible for processing visual data.
It consists of a microelectrode array embedded in the visual cortex capable of stimulating neurons.

title: 3D and 4D Printing Technologies
focus: Additive manufacturing processes, advantages, applications across sectors, and the distinction of 4D printing.
rationale: Core emerging technology topic with broad applications and a clear conceptual distinction between 3D (additive) and 4D (time/stimuli-responsive) printing.
sectionExcerpt: 3D printing, or additive manufacturing, creates three-dimensional objects from digital files by laying down successive layers of material. It offers customization, cost-effectiveness, and applications in healthcare (bioprinting), forensics, and manufacturing. 4D printing evolves this by adding the dimension of time, allowing objects to change shape or function in response to environmental stimuli like heat or moisture.
evidenceBullets: 3D printing is an additive process creating objects by laying down successive layers of material.
Applications of 3D printing include bioprinting organs (organ on chip), reconstructing fossils, and forensic pathology.
4D printing evolved from 3D printing by adding the dimension of time.
4D printed objects can change shape or function over time in response to environmental stimuli such as heat, light, or moisture.

title: Stablecoins and Cryptocurrency Regulation in India
focus: Definition and types of stablecoins, concerns, and India's taxation and regulatory framework for Virtual Digital Assets (VDAs).
rationale: High-yield economy and tech intersection. Covers definitions of stablecoins, types, and crucial Indian regulatory milestones (Finance Act 2022, PMLA 2002).
sectionExcerpt: Stablecoins are cryptocurrencies pegged to another currency, commodity, or financial instrument (e.g., Tether). Types include fiat-collateralized, crypto-collateralized, and algorithmic. In India, crypto assets are unregulated but taxed. The Finance Act 2022 imposed a 30% tax on capital gains from Virtual Digital Assets (VDAs), and in 2023, VDAs were brought under the Prevention of Money-laundering Act (PMLA), 2002.
evidenceBullets: Stablecoins are pegged to currencies, commodities, or financial instruments to maintain value.
There are three primary types of stablecoins: fiat-collateralized, crypto-collateralized, and non-collateralized (algorithmic).
India imposed a 30% tax on capital gains from Virtual Digital Assets (VDAs) via the Finance Act, 2022.
In 2023, VDAs were brought under the purview of the Prevention and Money-laundering Act, 2002.
Cryptocurrency is based on blockchain, a distributed public ledger, and created via mining.

title: Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) and Digital Rupee
focus: Concept of CBDC, advantages over stablecoins (sovereign backing, cross-border efficiency), and features of India's Digital Rupee.
rationale: Important macroeconomic and digital finance topic. Contrasts CBDC with stablecoins and details the RBI's Digital Rupee.
sectionExcerpt: CBDC is a digital form of sovereign currency issued by a central bank. It is preferred over stablecoins due to sovereign backing, legal tender status, and efficiency in cross-border payments (reducing reliance on SWIFT). India's CBDC is the Digital Rupee, issued by the RBI, offering features similar to physical cash including finality of settlement and RBI guarantee.
evidenceBullets: CBDC is a digital form of money issued by a central bank and recognized as legal tender.
CBDCs offer sovereign backing and operate within a regulated framework, unlike many stablecoins.
CBDCs can simplify international trade payments and reduce reliance on intermediaries like SWIFT.
The Digital Rupee is India's CBDC, issued by the RBI.
Digital Rupee offers features similar to physical cash, including the guarantee of the RBI and finality of settlement.
